analysis.cudnn_dispatch_note: nn.LSTM compiled with torch.compile+inductor on PyTorch 2.11/H100 stays as a single cuDNN operator (aten::_cudnn_rnn). Inductor does NOT decompose LSTM into per-timestep Triton kernels on this configuration. FX graph passes cannot reach inside cuDNN. All optimizations are non-graph transformations applied before torch.compile.

operators: aten::_cudnn_rnn (all 10 measurement-iteration instances, 98.9% of total time)

bottleneck.description: cuDNN LSTM dispatches to 'Kernel2' — the legacy FP32 SIMT GEMM path — because model inputs are FP32. On H100, BF16/FP16 inputs cause cuDNN to select sm90_xmma_gemm_bf16bf16_* (WGMMA, full Tensor Core path), which delivers 4x the throughput of SIMT for this workload.

evidence.kernel_meaning: cuDNN internal non-HMMA gate GEMM for FP32 LSTM
evidence.dtype: FP32 (inferred from model defaults, confirmed by sm80 addmm kernel for linear head)
evidence.h100_dispatch_rule: cuDNN routes LSTM to WGMMA (sm90_xmma_gemm_bf16bf16_*) only when input dtype is BF16 or FP16. FP32 always uses Kernel2 (SIMT path, no Tensor Core utilization).
evidence.metric_availability: tensor_core_active_pct=null (cuDNN opaque to ncu); bottleneck inferred from kernel name + dtype

transformation.description: Cast model parameters and input tensor to BF16. This is a non-graph transformation applied in get_model_and_input() before torch.compile. cuDNN responds to the dtype of the input tensor and weight buffers at runtime: BF16 inputs trigger selection of sm90_xmma_gemm_bf16bf16_* (WGMMA Tensor Core path on H100).
transformation.code_hint: Apply BEFORE torch.compile(). BF16 is preferred over FP16 for H100 (native BF16 accumulation in WGMMA, no overflow risk for hidden states). Do NOT use FP16 for LSTM — sigmoid/tanh activations require sufficient dynamic range.

estimated_impact.throughput_multiplier: 2-4x on aten::_cudnn_rnn (SIMT → WGMMA; H100 BF16 GEMM throughput 3989 TOPS vs. ~1000 TOPS for FP32 SIMT at this tile size)
estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: ~2x (BF16 halves weight and activation DRAM footprint for the LSTM cell)

optimizations.notes: BF16 for LSTM is well-supported in cuDNN since PyTorch 1.9. Hidden states accumulate in BF16 across 128 timesteps; numerical precision is sufficient for inference but may degrade training loss at high learning rates. This workload is eval() mode — BF16 is safe.

bottleneck.description: cuDNN selects LSTM algorithm statically based on input shape at first call. torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark=True enables cuDNN to benchmark multiple algorithm candidates (including new BF16-optimized algorithms added in recent cuDNN versions) and cache the fastest one for this exact (batch=32, seq=128, input=256, hidden=512) configuration.

evidence.benchmark_opportunity: Without benchmark=True, cuDNN uses a heuristic to select algorithm. With benchmark=True after BF16 cast, cuDNN profiles available sm90 LSTM algorithms and picks the fastest for the current hardware and input shape.

transformation.description: Enable cuDNN benchmark mode to auto-select the fastest LSTM algorithm for this input shape. Apply after BF16 cast (OPT-1) so cuDNN benchmarks BF16-specific algorithms, not FP32 ones. Also enable TF32 for the linear classifier head (aten::addmm).
transformation.code_hint: Set these flags at module level or in get_model_and_input() before the model is constructed. cudnn.benchmark=True has a one-time warmup cost (first forward is slow while cuDNN benchmarks); subsequent calls use the cached algorithm. This is already handled by the --warmup-iters=5 in the profiling pipeline.

estimated_impact.throughput_multiplier: 1.05-1.2x additional on top of OPT-1 (algorithm selection within BF16 path)

optimizations.notes: Must be applied AFTER OPT-1 (BF16 cast) to benchmark BF16 algorithms. If applied before BF16 cast, cuDNN will cache a FP32 algorithm and may not re-benchmark when dtype changes (cached plans are dtype-keyed so this should be safe, but ordering ensures clean benchmark).

bottleneck.description: The linear classifier (addmm, [32x512]x[512x10]) dispatches to sm80_xmma_gemm_f32f32_f32f32_f32_tn_n_tilesize32x32x8_stage3 in FP32. After BF16 cast (OPT-1), the addmm will dispatch to a BF16 path. The 'tn' layout suffix means the weight is transposed at call time (aten.t() on the weight tensor). Pre-transposing eliminates this per-call transpose.

evidence.tn_suffix: 'tn' = transposed-N layout; weight is transposed on the fly each forward pass

transformation.description: Pre-transpose the classifier Linear weight and store as a buffer to eliminate the aten.t() call. After BF16 cast, the pre-transposed buffer is also BF16. This is an FX graph pass applied inside the custom backend.
transformation.code_hint: At 0.7% of total time, impact is negligible (<0.2ms). Include as a completeness pass but do not expect measurable speedup.

optimizations.notes: Impact is negligible (0.04% of total time). Included for completeness — demonstrates that FX passes are running correctly even when the dominant bottleneck is cuDNN-opaque. If the FX pattern does not match after Inductor tracing (addmm shape may be fused differently), the pass degrades gracefully with a WARNING log.

global_notes: All duration values from ncu kernel-replay mode — 2-5x longer than real wall-clock execution. Use only for relative comparison between baseline and optimized profiles.
H100 PCIe: 114 SMs, 2TB/s HBM3 bandwidth, 3989 TOPS BF16 Tensor Core (WGMMA). Ridge point ~200 FLOP/byte BF16.

CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL NOTE: nn.LSTM with torch.compile+inductor on PyTorch 2.11/H100 stays as aten::_cudnn_rnn (a single cuDNN kernel). Inductor does NOT decompose LSTM into per-timestep Triton kernels on this system. This means: (1) FX graph passes see only the linear head and mean pooling, not LSTM gate operations; (2) All LSTM optimization must be done non-graph (dtype, config flags); (3) The profile's limited counter coverage (Null values for cuDNN ops) is expected and correct.
LSTM gate GEMM shapes: each timestep [32, 768] x [768, 2048] for layer 1, [32, 1024] x [1024, 2048] for layer 2. These are substantial GEMMs that benefit significantly from Tensor Core utilization.
